Section 5.3 Improper Integrals Involving Rational and Exponential Functions
Because of this fact, we can compute a convergent integral over the line by computing its principal value, which can often be obtained by us complex methods and the residue theorem.

How can we know if an integral is convergent? We can use the follow comparison test.

ON 1
FOR

Let $\int_{A}^{B} f(x) d x$ represent an improper integral as in (1), where $A=-0 B=\infty$.

PER
ALS
(i) If $\int_{A}^{B}|f(x)| d x$ is convergent, then $\int_{A}^{B} f(x) d x$ is convergent and we $\left|\int_{A}^{B} f(x) d x\right| \leq \int_{A}^{B}|f(x)| d x$.
(ii) If $|f(x)| \leq g(x)$ for all $A<x<B$ and $\int_{A}^{B} g(x) d x$ is convergent, $\int_{A}^{B} f(x) d x$ is convergent and we have $\left|\int_{A}^{B} f(x) d x\right| \leq \int_{A}^{B} g(x) d x$.
Proof We prove part (i) in the case $A$ finite and $B=\infty$ and $f$ is real-valued. proof of the other cases is similar and will be omitted. For any $b>A$, using (3 Section 3.2, we have $\left|\int_{A}^{b} f(x) d x\right| \leq \int_{A}^{b}|f(x)| d x$. Given that $I=\int_{A}^{\infty}|f(x)| d x<$ consider the nonnegative function $|f(x)|-f(x)$. Since it is nonnegative, its integ is nonnegative and increases with the size of the interval of integration. That me that $F(b)=\int_{A}^{b}(|f(x)|-f(x)) d x$ is a nonnegative increasing function of $b$. Moreov $F(b)=\int_{A}^{b}(|f(x)|-f(x)) d x \leq 2 \int_{A}^{b}|f(x)| d x \leq 2 \int_{A}^{\infty}|f(x)| d x=2 I$. So, $F(b)$ nonnegative, increasing and bounded above; therefore it has a limit $L \geq 0$ as $b \rightarrow$ Now $L=\lim _{b \rightarrow \infty} \int_{A}^{b}(|f(x)|-f(x)) d x=\lim _{b \rightarrow \infty} \int_{A}^{b}|f(x)| d x-\lim _{b \rightarrow \infty} \int_{A}^{b} f(x) d x I-\lim _{b \rightarrow \infty} \int_{A}^{b} f(x) d x$. So, $\lim _{b \rightarrow \infty} \int_{A}^{b} f(x) d x=I-L$, which shows that the integ $\int_{A}^{\infty} f(x) d x=I-L$ is finite and $\leq I$, because $L \geq 0$. Repeating this argum with $|f(x)|+f(x)$ in place of $|f(x)|-f(x)$, we obtain that $-\int_{A}^{\infty} f(x) d x$ exists a is $\leq I$. Hence, $\left|\int_{A}^{\infty} f(x) d x\right| \leq \int_{A}^{\infty}|f(x)| d x$.

To prove (ii), let $A \leq b$ and note that $\int_{A}^{b}|f(x)| d x$ is an increasing function o because $|f(x)|$ is nonnegative. It is also bounded above by $\int_{A}^{\infty} g(x) d x$, because the inequality $|f(x)| \leq g(x)$, and so $\lim _{b \rightarrow \infty} \int_{A}^{b}|f(x)| d x$ exists and is $\leq \int_{A}^{\infty} g(x)$ Hence $\int_{A}^{\infty}|f(x)| d x$ is convergent, and from (i) it follows that $\int_{A}^{\infty} f(x) d x$ is conv gent and is $\leq \int_{A}^{\infty} g(x) d x$.

You are encouraged to use Proposition 1 or any other test from calcu (such as the limit comparison test) to show that an improper integral is cc vergent. Once the convergence is determined, we may compute the integ via its principal value, as we will illustrate in the examples.

EXAMPLE 1 Improper integrals and residues: the main ideas Evaluate
$$
I=\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \frac{x^{2}}{x^{4}+1} d x
$$

Solution To highlight the main ideas, we present the solution in basic steps. Step 1: Show that the improper integral is convergent. Because the integre is continuous on the real line, it is enough to show that the integral outsid
